# Mirodan PhD Vol 2 — Chapter II: Attitudes (extract)

Source: /Users/opaque/Downloads/Mirodan-PhD-1997-Vol2.pdf, printed pp. 365-523
Extractor: parallel chapter agent (priority chapter)
Status: in progress

## 0. POLAR VISITATIONS / MICRO-SHIFTS — priority extraction

### Mirodan's terminology: "FUSIONS" and "GOING"

Mirodan does NOT use "polar visitation" or "micro-shift." Her exact term is **"Fusions"**, and the verb construction is **"X going Y"** — e.g. "Strong/Bound going Strong/Free", "Light/Bound going Light/Free." The Bound→Free motion is the canonical case (Adream), and the Quick→Sustained / Sustained→Quick is the Mobile case.

### CANONICAL DEFINITION (p. 449-450, Remote section)

> "Fusions are appearances of Free Flow covering a Bound Flow inner. A character in Fusion tries to convince others that he has Free Flow, but in reality he is extremely Bound and that is apparent to everyone except himself." (p. 449-450)

This is the most important sentence in the chapter for the polar-visitation question. Mirodan's mechanism is: **the Free pole is a mask, the Bound pole is the truth, and the gap between them is visible to everyone except the character.** Fusions are not a state change — they are a Bound character "appearing as" Free, and failing.

### Scope: only Flow-Attitudes have Fusions

Mirodan only documents Fusions for the Inner Attitudes that contain **Flow** as part of their Action stress (Adream, Mobile, Remote). Why? Because Fusions are explicitly about Bound→Free flow — the axis is Flow, not Weight/Time/Space.

Confirmed in:
- **Adream Fusions** (p. 469): Strong/Bound going Strong/Free; Light/Bound going Light/Free
- **Remote Fusions** (p. 432, table; p. 449-450, prose): Direct/Bound going Direct/Free (Pressing going Dabbing); Flexible/Bound going Flexible/Free (Wringing going Flicking)
- **Mobile Fusions** (p. 477, table): Quick/Bound going Quick/Free (Slashing going Dabbing); Sustained/Bound going Sustained/Free (Wringing going Flicking)

NOT documented for Stable, Near, Awake (these have Space/Time/Weight stresses, not Flow).

> "Adreain, like all the Attitudes which contain Flow, is prone to Fusions. These are Strong/Bound going Strong/Free and Light/Bound going Light/Free." (p. 469)

**Critical scope rule**: Fusions live only on the Flow axis for Flow-Attitudes (Adream/Mobile via their inner Bound/Free oscillation). The Mobile Variations table at p. 477 names them too:

> "Quick/Bound going Quick/Free — Slashing going Dabbing
> Sustained/Bound going Sustained/Free — Wringing going Gliding" (Mobile Variations table, p. ~477)

### Mechanism — introvert Motif "going" extravert Motif

The Fusion is described as one Subconscious Motif (the introvert one, anchored to Bound) erupting outward as the extravert Motif (anchored to Free) of the same Weight pole. The character STAYS in the baseline Variation but the GESTURE swings outward.

Adream Strong/Bound (Pressing, introvert) "going" Strong/Free (Slashing, extravert):
> "the 'introvert' Subconscious Motif of Strong/Bound is Pressing, and the 'extravert' Subconscious Motif of Strong/Free is Slashing. Therefore, Strong/Bound going Strong/Free will manifest itself as Pressing going Slashing." (p. 469)

Mirodan's example (the adolescent slamming doors):
> "That is why, says Yat Malmgren, adolescents slam doors and stamp their feet, hit their fists on the table or slap rivals (or the equivalent actions in words) — these gestures are outer Slashing moves motivated by an inner Pressing. The adolescent-like character is angry, but at the bottom of his anger there is fear and a search for self-preservation. If he were to remain in his 'sombre' (Strong/Bound) mode and act with violent gestures, real Punches based on inner Pressing, he could kill in anger. This is socially unacceptable and dangerous, so such characters weaken the power of the anger through outer Free Flow, turning the Punch into a slap (Slashing)." (p. 469)

Adream Light/Bound (Gliding, introvert) "going" Light/Free (Flicking, extravert):
> "Light/Bound going Light/Free will manifest itself in the Subconscious Motifs of Gliding going Flicking. Teenagers giggling for no apparent reason, actors corpsing on stage, undergo this kind of Fusion." (p. 469)

### Triggers — Fusion erupts under social/internal pressure

Mirodan's triggers cluster around: **social-impossibility of staying at the introvert pole** (would-kill anger → released as slap), **dual emotional pressure** (the wife in *La Ronde*: attraction + insecurity → Light/Bound going Light/Free), and **revealed feeling that cannot be sustained inwardly** (teenage giggle, actor corpsing).

> "The coy young wife becomes more and more insecure, Adream Light/Bound going Light/Free under the dual pressure of her attraction for him and her insecurity about his real feelings." (p. 470)

### Duration — moment, not state

Crucially, the Fusion is described as a moment of release, not a re-anchoring. The character returns to baseline (Bound) afterward. The Mobile case is most explicit:

> "Carpenter is clear that the Mobility lies in the moment of revelation, not in the enjoyment which precedes and follows it" (p. ~497, Mobile Sustained/Free section)

This means Fusions are **episodic eruptions**, not state changes. The Bound-anchored character momentarily projects through Free flow, then snaps back. The phrase "going" carries kinetic, transient sense — a passage, not a transition.

### Returns — why the snap-back

Snap-back is implicit in Mirodan's framing: the introvert Motif (Pressing/Gliding/Wringing) is the TRUE inner state, and the extravert Motif (Slashing/Flicking/Dabbing) is its socially-permitted release valve. The release dissipates the energy; the inner pole reasserts.

### Lear example — Fusion stacked across Stresses (pp. 470-473)

Mirodan's longest worked example. Lear cursing Goneril is Adream Strong/Bound (Punching/Pressing). Just before the curse, he greets Albany **Direct Attending** (Awake-flavored), turning the rage cold:

> "Here Lear brings Direct Attending to his Adream anger. This has the effect of turning the burning rage of the curse into a cold, deliberate spite. Attending produces Direct/Bound Remote and Direct/Strong Stable on the Outer Action. On the Inner Action there is a furious Mobility, constantly constructing and destroying with the idea of betrayal." (p. 471)

Then a Stress flip during the same scene:
> "Shakespeare allows him a second opportunity to change from Time-stress to Space-stress... thus turning Lear away from his Time-stressed fury and back to his original Space-stressed, more thoughtful, analytical mode. The control of Mobility afforded by the Space, allows the Near element in Adream to reassert itself and balance the Mobile out." (pp. 471-472)

Then ANOTHER swing back:
> "Lear's anger bursts out again, emotion sweeps away Thought and Weight, and he swings back to a Remote Inner and Near Outer Action, cursing Goneril again. Finally, at the extreme of the emotional swing of the pendulum, his Inner Action becomes Stable, 'dealing' in imagination with the problem of leadership and kingship, while his Outer Action is a ridiculous, pathetic, empty Mobile threat of revenge" (p. 472)

**Key insight from Lear**: Stress can flip mid-scene (Time-stress ↔ Space-stress). This is more than a Fusion — Mirodan calls it a "swing of the pendulum." The character's baseline Inner Attitude (Adream) holds; the STRESS axis migrates. This is a different mechanism than Fusion.

### Multi-Variation occupation — characters use 3 of 4 tempi

> "My examination of the four Adream Variations must not be taken to mean that Characters limit themselves to only one Variation at a time. On the contrary, most characters use at least three of the four 'tempi' open to them at the level of the Inner Attitude. Moreover, their Action has at its disposal two Stresses as well and Characters can switch between them within scenes, to create different relationships and different 'beats'." (p. 470)

This is the answer to "is a baseline character locked to one pole?" — NO. Mirodan: a character within an Inner Attitude has access to **three of the four Variations** typically, plus **both stresses** to switch between. The "switch between Stresses within scenes" creates "beats."

### Signature vs drift

Fusions are explicitly **signature** in Mirodan's analysis — they are the social-release mechanism written into the role (adolescent slam, teen giggle, coy wife). She frames them as part of the Character's repertoire, not as an actor's failure.

But there's a critical second reading from p. 449-450: the Free Flow is FAKE — the character "tries to convince others that he has Free Flow" but is "extremely Bound." So the Fusion is signature in being deliberately written into the character's social mask, but it functions as **transparent self-deception** — visible to audience and other characters as a fake.

### The four documented Fusion examples (consolidated)

| Inner Attitude | Variation | Inner Motif | Outer Motif | Mirodan example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Adream | Strong/Bound going Strong/Free | Pressing | Slashing | Adolescent slam, hit-fist, slap rivals (p. 469) |
| Adream | Light/Bound going Light/Free | Gliding | Flicking | Teenage giggle, actor corpsing, La Ronde wife (p. 469-470) |
| Remote | Direct/Bound going Direct/Free | Pressing | Dabbing | "Self-obsessed egocentric making visible efforts to be pleasant"; Maltese Falcon Greenfield character (p. 450) |
| Remote | Flexible/Bound going Flexible/Free | Wringing | Flicking | Society hostess "eaten inside with resentment but covering badly with casual flicks of ash, opening fan, twitches" (p. 450) |
| Mobile | Quick/Bound going Quick/Free | Slashing | Dabbing | (table p. 477) |
| Mobile | Sustained/Bound going Sustained/Free | Wringing | Flicking | (table p. 477) |

### Interpretation summary

- **Mirodan's terminology**: "Fusions" with "X going Y" verb form
- **Mechanism**: Bound (introvert) Subconscious Motif acts AS Free (extravert) Motif of same Weight pole
- **Truth-state**: Inner is Bound; outer is fake-Free; transparent to observers
- **Duration**: episodic (moment of revelation/release), not state change
- **Triggers**: social impossibility of expressing Bound truth (rage that would kill, resentment that would shame)
- **Returns**: implicit — the Free is dissipation, the Bound reasserts after release
- **Axis**: Flow only (Bound↔Free), not documented for Weight/Time/Space sub-axes
- **Signature/drift**: Mirodan treats them as character-signature, written into the role's social repertoire



## 1. Six Inner Attitudes — overview

### Definition (p. 372, "Book" quoted by Mirodan)

> "The six subconscious Inner Attitudes of:
> STABLE  [Intending/Attending or Attending/Intending]
> MOBILE  [Deciding/Adapting or Adapting/Deciding]
> NEAR    [Intending/Deciding or Deciding/Intending]
> REMOTE  [Attending/Adapting or Adapting/Attending]
> AWAKE   [Attending/Deciding or Deciding/Attending]
> ADREAM  [Intending/Adapting or Adapting/Intending]
> which are normally motivated in the sub-conscious mind, but which can be activated by bodily movements. Each Inner Attitude is revealed in our movements (expression) as a compound of two Elements." ("Book": p. 16, Mirodan p. 372)

### The two-part structure (p. 365-368)

Each Inner Attitude is built from **two complete Motion Factors** (not one Element). Single-element exaggerations create cartoon types ("Crampedness", "Sloppiness", "Obstinacy", "Fussiness", "Laziness", "Hastiness", "Flightiness", "Stickiness" — pp. 366-367). The Inner Attitudes are the next step up: realistic characters express through **two Motion Factors between whose yielding and contending Elements the energy flows freely**. The psychological counterpart is "an outlook on life, an Attitude" (p. 368).

### Labels and Mirodan's character types (p. 372)

| Inner Attitude | Laban's label | Mirodan's description | Mental Factor pair |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stable | "unadaptive" | "the intelligent ruler" | Sensing + Thinking (Intending/Attending) |
| Mobile | "adapting" | "highly emotional" | Feeling + Intuiting (Adapting/Deciding) |
| Near | "relating" | "down to earth" | Sensing + Intuiting (Intending/Deciding) |
| Remote | "solitude" | "ascetic" | Thinking + Feeling (Attending/Adapting) |
| Awake | "awareness" | "the intellectual" | Thinking + Intuiting (Attending/Deciding) |
| Adream | "unawareness" | "the lover" | Sensing + Feeling (Intending/Adapting) |

### Fundamentals vs derivatives (p. 373)

**Fundamental (one-centred)** — those containing Deciding/Intuiting:
- Near = pure Sensing
- Mobile = pure Feeling
- Awake = pure Thinking

**Derivative (two-centred)** — combinations:
- Near + Awake = **Stable** (purple)
- Near + Mobile = **Adream** (orange)
- Awake + Mobile = **Remote** (turquoise)

Postures Mirodan attributes (p. 373):
- Near creates width — like sitting astride a motorbike
- Awake elongates and makes flexible — like a tall reed
- Mobile floats — buffeted like a leaf in the wind

### Aspects (the emphasized side) (p. 374)

> "When Intending is emphasized the Aspect is **enclosing**.
> When Attending is emphasized the Aspect is **penetrating**.
> When Deciding is emphasized the Aspect is **circumscribing**.
> When Adapting is emphasized the Aspect is **radiating**." (p. 374)

The Aspect determines the primary Jungian "type" of the character. Mirodan illustrates with Othello (Adream enclosing — sensuality dominant) vs Desdemona (Adream radiating — feeling dominant) (p. 374).

### Lifecycle / archetypal mapping (pp. 376-377)

- At birth: three fundamental Inner Attitudes present (Near, Mobile, Awake)
- At puberty: ADREAM emerges (Intending + Adapting, marked by confirmation/bar-mitzvah)
- In professional maturity: STABLE emerges (Intending + Attending, marked by voting/marriage)
- In old age: REMOTE emerges (Attending + Adapting, marked by burial rites)

Biblical archetypes: Abraham = Stable, Isaac = Near, Jacob = Adream; Sarah = Stable, Rebecca = Adream enclosing, Leah = Near, Rachel = Adream radiating.

### Real people vs characters

> "real people (as opposed to characters) possess all the Inner Attitudes — the entire complex of psychological characteristics — but not in equal measure. We are biased naturally towards one or the other of the Inner Attitudes, to the detriment, but not the total exclusion, of the others." (p. 378)

Theatre characters, by contrast, have **fixed** Inner Attitudes — Mirodan: their conscious attitudes always spring from one unconscious cocktail. This is the whole basis for the analytic system applied to dramatic work.

### Three-of-six structure

Each character has access to **three Inner Attitudes** typically (the home one + two related):
> "The Sensing characters can be Stable or Adream as well as Near. He can also have the other two Sensing Inner Attitudes — Stable and Adream. Even then, real people have at least the potential for all six Inner Attitudes because... " (p. 379)

Mirodan: characters fall into one of the six baselines but can produce Outer Actions reaching the others through Stress mechanics (see section 8).


## 2. Stable — full treatment

### Definition (p. 487)

> "Stable (characteristic 'unadapting') is a combination of the Mental Factors of Sensing and Thinking which produce the Inner Participations of Intending/Attending. When Intending is emphasized, the Aspect is enclosing. When Attending is emphasized, the Aspect is penetrating. Its Action can be either Time-stressed or Flow-stressed."

### Stress → Action Attitudes (p. 487)

- TIME + WEIGHT = NEAR; AWAKE = TIME + SPACE
- FLOW + WEIGHT = ADREAM; REMOTE = FLOW + SPACE

### Variations (p. 487) — NO Fusions documented

| Variation | Label | Subconscious Motifs |
|---|---|---|
| Strong/Direct | "commanding" | Punching / Pressing |
| Light/Flexible | "receptive" | Floating / Flicking |
| Strong/Flexible | "practical" | Wringing / Slashing |
| Light/Direct | "self-contained" | Gliding / Dabbing |

(Stable's sub-axes are Weight × Space — no Bound/Free axis as primary, so no Fusions table.)

### Feel / posture / character (pp. 487-493)

> "Stable is a complex Inner Attitude, a combination of Near and Awake. Because it contains Weight (Intending), Stable can exist at the level of the Character. Stable characters have highly developed intellectual powers, which they direct with strong will... They always 'go somewhere'." (p. 487)

> "Stable characters appear in control of events and, by extension, are considered to have an elevated social or spiritual status. They are, says Yat Malmgren, like a powerful car, a Bentley or a Rolls-Royce: a lot of power contained under a smooth exterior and always giving the impression of knowing exactly where it is going." (p. 488)

Mirodan: Stable is generated by **moving away from Mobile**. "Stable can therefore be said to consist of a lack of Mobility... a character needs to have experienced and dismissed the sensuous and emotional excitements characteristic of youth. Only then does a character reach the plateau of inner calm which is described as Stability." (p. 489)

> "By discarding Mobility, Stable characters become devoid of Flow (Feeling) and Time (Intuition). Moving away from emotion, or rather 'solving', coming to terms with all emotional problems, is an essential formative experience for Stable characters... for Stable all important decisions have already been taken and it is only a matter of pursuing the chosen course to the end." (p. 489)

Posture: Stable images = oak trees, elephants, pyramids, moose alone in icy wasteland. "Free-standing." Stable "stands self-contained in a barren landscape." (p. 492)

Aspects:
- **Enclosing Stable** = Hawthorne in *The Crucible* — supremely "unadapting", inflexible, time-stressed brutality (p. 490)
- **Penetrating Stable** = the Duke in *Measure for Measure* — full of inner doubts, intellectual, Flow-stressed (p. 490-491)

Mirodan recommends "**unchangeable**" rather than Laban's "unadapting" — Stable becomes Stable through one absolute prior adapting that ends all subsequent adaptation.

### Variations detail

**STABLE STRONG/DIRECT ("commanding")** (p. 494):
> "Commanding demonstration or acceptance of a 'bold resolve'. Motifs: Punching/Pressing."

Edith Cavell statue. "Yes, yes, I agree!" (introvert) or extravert "demonstrating bold resolve." Most contending Stable Variation. Solness in *The Master Builder* (enclosing); Zarastro in *The Magic Flute* (penetrating).

**STABLE LIGHT/FLEXIBLE ("receptive")** (p. 495):
> "Receptive acceptance or receptive rejection of a 'welcoming tenderness'. Motifs: Floating / Flicking."

The Annunciation — Mary "accepts" Gabriel with "welcoming tenderness." "Receptive rejection" = positive flexible let-down ("a love affair coming to an end... where one is concerned not to hurt the other"). Countess of Rousillon in *All's Well*.

**STABLE STRONG/FLEXIBLE ("practical")** (p. 495-497):
> "Developing intention to cast or to submit to a 'spell-binding power'. Motifs: Wringing / Slashing."

Similar Interpretation to Adream Strong/Free ("irradiant"), but "developing" (not static-radiating). Queen Margaret in *Henry VI* — casting spell to restore courage. Dejaneira in *Women of Trachos* — accepts the truth of her husband's infidelity, then takes Stable controlled response. "Practical" Stable: "what can I do with these Given Circumstances?"

**STABLE LIGHT/DIRECT ("self-contained")** (p. 497-498):
> "Cautious expression or cautious acceptance of a 'gentle deference'. Motifs: Gliding / Dabbing."

Upright Victorian lady tempo. Melanie Wilkes welcoming Scarlett at the birthday party in *Gone With The Wind* — cautious acceptance of forbearance, dominating emotional waves by sticking to convention. Dickens' clerks: light Dabbing physically, nasty underneath.

### Stress detail (pp. 491-493)

**Time-stressed Stable** (e.g. Creon in *Antigone*) → enclosing, brutal, Near + Awake outers. "Rock-like Stable."

**Flow-stressed Stable** (e.g. Werle in *The Wild Duck*; the Duke in *Measure for Measure*) → softer, "more pensive", "almost feminine, flexible, yielding, introvert" Adream + Remote outers.

> "Flow-stressed Stable characters come across as softer, perhaps more sophisticated in their thinking, than their Time-stressed equivalents." (p. 491 footnote)

### Drives / inner quest

> "Stable characters tries to induce Mobility in others, to scare them with it or enthuse them through the central idea (the state, equality, the law, etc.) by which they themselves are dominated. Stable characters want to make everyone else adopt their 'great idea'." (p. 493)

### Fortinbras worked example (pp. 498-501)

Mirodan walks through Fortinbras' final speeches showing **all four Stable Variations + both Stresses operating within minutes**. He shifts from Strong/Direct (commanding "Let four captains bear Hamlet") through Strong/Flexible (introspective acknowledgment of Hamlet's qualities) to Light/Flexible (Free-Flow softening) to Light/Direct (penetrating, irritated). Action Attitudes oscillate between Awake Inner / Adream Outer (political calculation covered by admiration) and Near Inner / Remote Outer (possessive greed covered by distance). 

Yat Malmgren's punchline: stability over a Mobile situation is established by the **multitude of relationships** with stage points of contact — past, future, audience, soldiers, Hamlet, "I", captains. Each relationship swings the actor through different Variations.



## 3. Mobile — full treatment

### Definition (p. 503)

> "Mobile (characteristic 'adaptive') is a combination of the Mental Factors of Feeling and Intuiting which produce the Inner Participations of Adapting/Deciding. When Adapting is emphasized, the Aspect is referred to as 'radiating'. When Deciding is emphasized the Aspect is referred to as 'circumscribing'. The Action can be either Weight-stressed or Space-stressed."

### Stress → Action Attitudes (p. 503)

- Flow + Weight = ADREAM; REMOTE = Flow + Space
- Time + Weight = NEAR; AWAKE = Time + Space

### Mobile is not a Character (p. 503-504 footnote)

> "Mobile, like Awake and Remote, not having Intending as a component, can only exist as an Action Attitude itself. We therefore speak of Mobile 'states' or 'tempi', not of Mobile Inner Attitudes or Characters... Mobile can only exist as an aspect of the Action of Characters which are either Adream (Time-stressed) or Near (Flow-stressed). It cannot exist as an aspect of Stable, its opposite Attitude, with which it is incompatible."

Mobile-Stable are mutually exclusive opposites.

### Variations + Fusions (p. 503)

| Variation | Label | Subconscious Motifs |
|---|---|---|
| Quick/Bound | "unacknowledged" | Punching / Slashing |
| Sustained/Free | "acknowledged" | Floating / Gliding |
| Quick/Free | "revealed" | Dabbing / Flicking |
| Sustained/Bound | "concealed" | Pressing / Wringing |

**Fusions** (p. 503):
- Quick/Bound going Quick/Free → Slashing going Dabbing
- Sustained/Bound going Sustained/Free → Wringing going Gliding

### Feel / posture / character (pp. 504-513)

> "Mobile is a combination of emotion and intuition. As it has no Weight, it is a state, not a character who can be embodied physically on stage. However, because its essence is unadulterated, overwhelming emotion, it is one of the most rewarding, and often the most sought after, of acting states." (p. 504)

Mobile metaphor: a 'mobile' sculpture — wind blows it, character "feels suspended in the air, turning and twisting with every thought" (p. 505).

> "In Mobile, Yat Malmgren says, Intending is thrust out of the Inner Attitude by the centrifugal force of the emotion. As a result, a character in a Mobile state aspires towards outside Weight, clings to Weight like ivy to the 'Near' oak tree, defends itself with Weight on the Outer Action." (p. 508)

Posture/dynamics:
- "A Free Mobile state opens up ('scatters', Laban says) while the opposite, Bound Mobility, tightens the joints and muscles of the body ('gathers' in Laban's terms)." (p. 513)
- "The spine is affected, as Mobile resides in the nervous centres, not in the muscles. As a result of Mobility, characters 'lose their legs', get 'weak in the arms' — the limbs collapse and the expression comes directly from the centre, from the spine — Mobile swings inside the body like a pendulum." (p. 513)

Mobile attracts to water (Ophelia, Abigail in *The Crucible*). Mobile in crowds = Bachantae frenzy, mob in *Enemy of the People*.

Aspects:
- **Radiating Mobile** = lived emotion, "warm glow" (Liv Ullman as Anna Christie)
- **Circumscribing Mobile** = displayed/affected emotion (Moliere's little marquis flapping in love or fright)

### Variations detail

**MOBILE QUICK/BOUND ("unacknowledged")** (p. 514-515):
> "Concealed sudden decision accepting or denying an 'unacknowledged feeling'. Motifs: Punching / Slashing."

The "shake it all off" tempo. Jerking head, lifting shoulders. Two modes: "denying" the unacknowledged feeling (extravert "Who? Me? Never! How dare you?!") or "accepting" (introvert "It's all my fault!"). Claudius' repentance speech full of Quick/Bound swings. Most contending Mobile variation.

**MOBILE SUSTAINED/FREE ("acknowledged")** (p. 515-518):
> "Slow revelation of enjoying or evading 'affection'. Motifs: Floating / Gliding."

Bliss/Nirvana. Carpenter's "affection" — Mirodan reads as "admiration, faith, belief." When you admire, you melt, yield. The tourist "melting" before the Venus de Milo. *The Measures Taken*: Young Comrade's "I agree" before being killed = "supreme act of submission." Romeo at the balcony scene end "lifts" off the ground.

> "Carpenter is clear that the Mobility lies in the moment of 'revelation' itself... The Mobile state occurs in the moment of revelation, not in the enjoyment which precedes and follows it... It is the idea, the realization of one's own enjoyment ('My God, she loves me...; My God, I've passed the exam') which is Mobile, not the states of relief or of happy exhaustion which precede it." (p. 517)

**MOBILE QUICK/FREE ("revealed")** (p. 518-519):
> "Sudden revelation expressing or rejecting an 'ardent sympathy'. Motifs: Dabbing / Flicking."

Skipping-teen tempo. "Twinkle twinkle little star" tune is built around three Dabbings. Gilda's love aria in *Rigoletto* — Light/Flexible Flicking moves. "Energy goes towards the inner revelation, that is towards or away from the idea of love — and not towards the outer object." Underpinned by yielding Sustained/Free.

**MOBILE SUSTAINED/BOUND ("concealed")** (p. 520-521):
> "A slow decision in favour or against a 'frigid self-assertion'. Motifs: Wringing / Pressing."

The guilt tempo. Inner layer of Quick/Bound (silent guilt → quick fearful Punches/Slashes). "Mea culpa, mea culpa, mea maxima culpa" with breast-beating. "Frigid" because the realization is gradual, deliberate, lacking the warmth of Quick/Bound revelation. Blanche du Bois's "intimacies with strangers" speech — Sustained/Bound at its purest.

### Fusions detail (p. 522)

> "Characters with Mobile Attitudes are also prone to Fusions. In Mobile Fusions Sustained/Bound goes Sustained/Free and Quick/Bound goes Quick/Free."

**Quick/Bound going Quick/Free** = "Slashing going Dabbing." Richard II's "Alack, how is't that I am called before a King..." — "Richard pretends to be puzzled — he is 'playing a game' — the essence of Fusion." Inner Slashing (cut opposition to quick) covered by ineffectual little pointing gestures (Dabbing) with chin, eyes, finger.

**Sustained/Bound going Sustained/Free** = "Wringing going Gliding." Characters who feel guilty but cover with frozen broad smiles.

### Drives / Quest

> "Mobile is the ability to change. The ability to enter in a Mobile state is therefore essential for the process of transformation." (p. 513)

Super-Objectives are forms of "hidden Mobile 'ideas' which we carry within us all our lives... they involve a fundamental adaptation of the psyche to the objective world." Route into Mobility = "personal image" (one's "room 101" in Orwell's *1984*).

> "Laban and Carpenter labelled Mobile 'adaptive'. The Mobile personality slavishly adjusts its inner state to the demands of the outside world. The supremely adaptive 'actor' is the medium." (p. 513)

### Johnny Boyle worked example (pp. 522-523)

Adream character whose Feeling overwhelms Sensing under threat of death. Mirodan tracks his speech through three of four Mobile Variations: Quick/Bound terror Punches → Sustained/Bound Wringing (the lie about his arm) → Sustained/Free prayer (collapse, "Sacred Heart of Jesus, have mercy on me!"). The unused Variation = Quick/Free ("ecstatic" — would be embracing death, which Johnny refuses).


## 4. Near — full treatment

### Definition (p. 409)

> "Near (characteristic 'relating') is a combination of the Mental Factors of Sensing and Intuiting which produce the Inner Participations of Intending/Deciding. When Intending is emphasized, the Aspect is enclosing. When Deciding is emphasized the Aspect is circumscribing. Its Action can be either Space-stressed or Flow-stressed."

### Stress → Action Attitudes (p. 409)

- SPACE + WEIGHT = STABLE; AWAKE = SPACE + TIME
- FLOW + WEIGHT = ADREAM; MOBILE = FLOW + TIME

(So a Near baseline character with Space-stress projects Outer Actions of STABLE and AWAKE. With Flow-stress, projects ADREAM and MOBILE.)

### The four Variations (p. 409)

| Variation | Label | Subconscious Motifs |
|---|---|---|
| Strong/Quick | "materialistic" | Punching / Slashing |
| Light/Sustained | "human" | Floating / Gliding |
| Strong/Sustained | "warm" | Pressing / Wringing |
| Light/Quick | "cool" | Dabbing / Flicking |

### Feel / posture / character (pp. 410-417)

> "Near characters react to the world through their senses. They continuously take decisions about their sensations: they place intuitively every new sensation in a 'box' alongside other, similar experiences classified in neat mental drawers and files." (p. 410)

Postural: width, horizontal axis. "Near has a tendency to spread out to the sides... Near characters often swing from side to side when they move, goose-like, with a rolling gait." (p. 416)

Aspects:
- **Enclosing Near** = the peasant, the earthy, sensuous, weighty (Big Daddy, Juno, Captain Boyle, Brecht's workers).
- **Circumscribing Near** = the conventional/imitative type (Casaubon, Mrs. Elvsted, the Nurse in *Romeo and Juliet*; soldiers; clerks; politicians; "The Maids").

Near's "common sense" = "intuitive decisions taken about sensation" (p. 411). Near is "blinkered — they can only see what is in front of their noses" (p. 414). Near sees objects, not symbols, "a spade's a spade" (p. 414).

Mirodan's heat metaphor: Near characters are "radiators... a whole range of heat waves, from 'cool' to 'hot'" — Mel Gibson hot, Faye Dunaway cool (p. 415).

### Variation detail

**NEAR STRONG/QUICK ("materialistic")** (p. 423-426):
> "A sudden aggressive intention towards or away from a 'materialistic desire'. The Subconscious Motifs of Strong/Quick are Slashing and Punching." ("Book" p. 22)

The most extravert Near tempo. "Having guts." Swear words are Strong/Quick. Outer-aspect: extravert Working Actions (Punching/Slashing). Mirodan key insight: **"Strong/Quick is more often than not the expression of a Strong/Sustained deeper inner tempo"** (p. 424). Natasha's example: outer Strong/Quick over inner Strong/Sustained — visible cake-icing of "niceness" hiding rapacious intent.

**NEAR LIGHT/SUSTAINED ("human")** (p. 426-427):
> "Slow decision to express or reject a 'gentle tenderness'. The Subconscious Motifs are Floating and Gliding."

The "nice" suburban tempo. The "rejection" is not dislike — it's "going-away from" the tenderness within the same Variation. Phrase split: "I would love to come...but I can't on Sunday" — first half toward, second half away, but both Light/Sustained.

**NEAR STRONG/SUSTAINED ("warm")** (p. 427-429):
> "A staunch intention of 'warm consent' or of 'warm dissent'. The Subconscious Motifs are Pressing and Wringing."

Introvert / background of Strong/Quick (Pressing precedes Punching). Chebutykin "Pie? Splendid!" example. Either "warm consent" toward materialistic desire or "warm dissent" away. With Bound Flow becomes the Lady Bracknell-style imperious disparaging.

**NEAR LIGHT/QUICK ("cool")** (p. 429-431):
> "Sudden pert intention of 'intimacy' or 'estrangement'. The Subconscious Motifs are Dabbing and Flicking."

The character "distances himself from what is happening around him." Cecily in *Importance of Being Earnest* is the prime example — pert, attacking, biting Dabbing "like a hen pecking." "Intimacy" = energy toward object; "estrangement" = energy toward actor.

### Negatives / drives

> "An excess of Nearness overwhelms the emotion. This conflict with all aspects of Feeling is one of the chief characteristics of Near." (p. 420)

Drive: materialistic dreams ("luxury, fast cars, beautiful women in fur coats... money!"). Near intuition: "limited to Deciding — to 'boxing' reality into abstract categories... does not rise above the mundane" (p. 414 footnote).

### Inner Quest / "characteristic"

Laban's label: "relating." Near is strongly affected by relationships, particularly familial — sensuous and conventional simultaneously. "Robotical in our family relations." Near relationships are "chummy"; with social superiors, the closeness "neutralizes" — superior is treated Remote, not Near (p. 421).

Greek chorus = "ultimate example of a Near relationship... 'one voice', the 'voice of common sense'" (p. 422).


## 5. Remote — full treatment

### Definition (p. 432)

> "Remote (characteristic 'solitude') is a combination of the Mental Factors of Thinking and Feeling which produce the Inner Participations of Attending/Adapting. When Attending is emphasized, the Aspect is penetrating. When Adapting is emphasized, the Aspect is radiating. Its Action can be either Weight-stressed or Time-stressed."

### Stress → Action Attitudes (p. 432)

- WEIGHT + SPACE = STABLE; ADREAM = WEIGHT + FLOW
- TIME + SPACE = AWAKE; MOBILE = TIME + FLOW

### CRITICAL: Remote is not a "Character" (p. 432 footnote)

> "The 'Book' speaks of Remote 'Action Attitudes' — these are theoretical notions only... Remote, like Awake and Mobile, not having Intending as a component, can only exist as an Action Attitude. We therefore speak of Remote 'states' or 'tempi', not of Remote Inner Attitudes or Characters... Remote can only exist as an aspect of the Action of characters which are either Adream (Space-stressed) or Stable (Flow-stressed). It cannot exist as an aspect of Near, its opposite Attitude, with which it is incompatible."

This is THE core rule for Remote: **Remote is achieved only via Adream-Space-stress or Stable-Flow-stress**. A pure Near character cannot reach Remote — Near and Remote are mutually exclusive opposites.

### Variations + Fusions (p. 432)

| Variation | Label | Subconscious Motifs |
|---|---|---|
| Direct/Bound | "egocentric" | Punching / Pressing |
| Flexible/Free | "altruistic" | Floating / Flicking |
| Direct/Free | "sociable" | Gliding / Dabbing |
| Flexible/Bound | "unsociable" | Wringing / Slashing |

**Fusions** (table on p. 432):
- Direct/Bound going Direct/Free → Pressing going Dabbing
- Flexible/Bound going Flexible/Free → Wringing going Flicking

### Feel / posture / character (pp. 433-442)

> "Remote does not concern itself with physical realities — it deals only with ideas and problems and these are intangible." (p. 433)

> "Yat Malmgren introduces Remote to his students by reminding them of the experience of silent, absorbed study. Such intellectual concentration cuts out all outside distractions... this 'distance' is the core feeling of the Remote state." (p. 433)

The actor seeking Remote must "find an intellectual concept which can produce emotion without having recourse to emotional memory" — because emotional memory operates through the senses, and "Sensing is inimical to the very essence of Remote" (p. 432).

> "Remote ideas are not to be confused with reactions to personal hurt — 'my girlfriend left me' is not a Remote-inducing idea. Remote is induced by ideas of the philosophical, political or religious type." (p. 432)

Voice: "needs a voice capable of running up and down the entire dynamic range... Remote sounds hollow, weightless, colourless — it has the disembodied resonance of the tomb." (p. 433)

Aspects:
- **Penetrating Remote** = Thinking type (cold, calculating arrogance — Dombey hiring the nanny, taking away her name, p. 436)
- **Radiating Remote** = Feeling type, Action of an Adream character (Frankford in *A Woman Killed With Kindness*, p. 437)

Image: "creating distance... a 'vacuum' in the kinesphere, by removing itself from all Nearness." Nietzsche quote: "what is creative does not take place in the market place. In the market place the fleas bite you." (p. 441)

### Variations detail

**REMOTE DIRECT/BOUND ("egocentric")** (p. 442-443):
> "Narrowing egocentric withdrawal into or rejection of an 'unsociable solitude'. The Subconscious Motifs are Punching and Pressing."

The Character feels at the centre of the universe, indifferent to others. Hamlet's "To be or not to be." Bound Flow Remoteness "comes out as sarcasm... Irony and sarcasm create a tangible distance." Jimmy Porter — Adream attacking through Remote sarcasm.

**REMOTE FLEXIBLE/FREE ("altruistic")** (p. 444-446):
> "Embracing altruistic feeling for or against a 'sociable cordiality'. The Subconscious Motifs are Floating and Flicking."

Most extravert variation. Heroes saving mankind/animals/the Party. "Perfect gentlemen, generous hosts, selfless lovers." Prospero's "Hast thou which art but air a touch, a feeling..." — Flexible/Free convoluted upward spiral.

**REMOTE DIRECT/FREE ("sociable")** (p. 447):
> "Developing or contracting feeling of 'sociable companionship'. The Subconscious Motifs are Gliding and Dabbing."

Real manners, genuine concern. Not pub gregariousness (that's Near). Can use teasing positive sarcasm.

**REMOTE FLEXIBLE/BOUND ("unsociable")** (p. 447-449):
> "Growing reflective image of a 'welcome solitude' or of an 'unwelcome solitude'. The Subconscious Motifs are Wringing and Slashing."

Mirodan: "Growing" should really read "narrowing" — Bound Flow has constrictive effect. "Reflective" = brooding. "Welcome solitude" = happy alone (English upper class haughtiness, Lady Bracknell). Richard II's "Alack, why am I sent for to a king" — convoluted Flexible/Bound sarcasm.

### Fusions detail (p. 449-450) — already extracted in section 0

> "The Remote Variations of Direct/Bound and Flexible/Bound are also subject to Fusions. We may recall that Fusions are appearances of Free Flow covering a Bound Flow inner. A character in Fusion tries to convince others that he has Free Flow, but in reality he is extremely Bound and that is apparent to everyone except himself."

Direct/Bound going Direct/Free: "self-obsessed egocentric making visible efforts to be pleasant or sociable... mostly comical, but it can be unctuously sinister, like that of the character played by Edward Greenfield in The Maltese Falcon."

Flexible/Bound going Flexible/Free: "society hostess eaten inside with resentment or envy but covering her feelings (badly) with a series of casual flicks of ash in the ashtray, closing and opening of the fan or twitches from the corner of the mouth."

Closing example: Rosita in Lorca's *Dona Rosita the Spinster* — "approaches the impossible goal of true inner Remoteness." Speech analysis shows her moving between all four Remote Variations within one monologue (Flexible/Bound Wringing, Direct/Bound Punching, Pressing release, Bound→Free transition).


## 6. Awake — full treatment

### Definition (p. 474)

> "Awake (characteristic 'awareness') is a combination of the Mental Factors of Thinking and Intuiting which produce the Inner Participations of Attending/Deciding. When Attending is emphasized, the Aspect is penetrating. When Deciding is emphasized, the Aspect is circumscribing. Its Action can be either Weight-stressed or Flow-stressed."

### Stress → Action Attitudes (p. 474)

- SPACE + WEIGHT = STABLE; NEAR = TIME + WEIGHT
- SPACE + FLOW = REMOTE; MOBILE = TIME + FLOW

### Awake is not a "Character" (p. 474 footnote)

> "Awake (like Mobile and Remote), not having Intending as a component, can only exist as an Action Attitude... Awake only appears as a component of the Action of either a Stable (Time-stressed) or a Near (Space-stressed) character. It cannot exist as an aspect of Adream, its opposite Attitude, with which it is incompatible."

Awake-Adream are mutually exclusive opposites, just like Near-Remote and Stable-Mobile.

### Variations (p. 474) — NO Fusions documented

| Variation | Label | Subconscious Motifs |
|---|---|---|
| Direct/Quick | "acute" | Punching / Dabbing |
| Flexible/Sustained | "doubting" | Floating / Wringing |
| Direct/Sustained | "certain" | Pressing / Gliding |
| Flexible/Quick | "uncertain" | Flicking / Slashing |

NOTE: Awake's table does NOT list Fusions (it has no Bound/Free axis as primary — its sub-axes are Direct/Flexible × Quick/Sustained, ie Space and Time).

### Feel / posture / character (pp. 474-479)

> "Awake is the name given by Carpenter to pure intellectual ability. Awake deals with ideas — it is pure thinking in action... characters with Awake ability are master logicians, their thinking does not drift towards the intuitive. On the contrary, they deal mainly with facts." (p. 474)

> "Awake is solid, static, light and transparent: like glass, like crystal, like diamonds. Awake eliminates both the sensuous and the emotional responses aroused by the objective world and replaces them with thought. This is why characters in Awake mode give the feeling of being sensuously cold and emotionally neutral... Characters seized by Awake give the impression of being supremely self-contained." (p. 475)

Posture: gaunt, bird-like (Brother William); head-emphasis, eyes "stare." Penetrating Awake = owl. Circumscribing Awake = stork. Color = light blue.

> "In Space Awake has the effect of drawing the body upwards, towards the head and, above it, towards the world of lofty ideas." (p. 479)

Aspects:
- **Penetrating Awake** = "see through" everyday things to the underlying idea (Sherlock Holmes, Brother William in *The Name of the Rose*, Talleyrand, Palmerston)
- **Circumscribing Awake** = thought as a cover, "transparent fake thinking" (Stoppard's George in *Jumpers*; Columbo — actually Near, fake-Awake to put pressure on suspects)

### Variations detail

**AWAKE DIRECT/QUICK ("acute")** (p. 480):
> "Acute sudden decision in favour of or against an 'idea'. Motifs: Punching/Dabbing."

The "Got it!" moment. Most contending Awake variation. Underpinned by Direct/Sustained ("certainty"). Mirodan: "the actor who wishes to arouse a Direct/Quick state must ask the question: 'what am I certain about?'"

**AWAKE FLEXIBLE/SUSTAINED ("doubting")** (p. 481):
> "Slowly dawning attention towards or away from a 'doubting awareness'. Motifs: Floating / Wringing."

Most yielding Awake variation. Doubt usually toward inner-emerging ideas, not outer. Long phrases on one breath. Saint Joan's "If I went into England against the will of God..."

**AWAKE DIRECT/SUSTAINED ("certain")** (p. 481-483):
> "Slow dawning of a 'certain awareness' or of an 'uncertain awareness'. Motifs: Pressing / Gliding."

The seized inner certainty. Galina Ulanova's arabesque-pique held in absolute stillness as Juliet — saying "Yes" with absolute certainty, then melting into Free Flow.

**AWAKE FLEXIBLE/QUICK ("uncertain")** (p. 483-484):
> "Sudden embracing image of a 'new idea' or of a 'new problem'. Motifs: Flicking / Slashing."

"But wouldn't it be better if we did it like that?" New-idea grasp. Auden's Herod speech — "And for me personally at this moment it would mean that God has given me the power to destroy Himself..." rolling between Flexible/Quick and Direct/Sustained.

### Negatives / drives

> "Awake can be seen as a lack of sensuality and emotion. As a result, Awake does not radiate warmth." (p. 479)

> "the Awake function in itself — as opposed to Awake characters — is not very useful as a training tool in the development of the actor." (p. 479)

Drive: ideas, facts, "what's the idea behind all these phenomena?" Recourse to deliberate conscious memory (vs Near's instinctive memory).


## 7. Adream — full treatment

### Definition (p. 453)

> "Adream (characteristic 'unawareness') is a combination of the Mental Factors of Sensing and Feeling which produce the Inner Participations of Intending/Adapting. When Intending is emphasized, the Aspect is enclosing. When Adapting is emphasized the Aspect is radiating. Its Action can be either Time-stressed or Space-stressed."

### Stress → Action Attitudes (p. 453)

- Weight + Time = NEAR; STABLE = Weight + Space
- Flow + Time = MOBILE; REMOTE = Flow + Space

### Variations + Fusions (p. 453)

| Variation | Label | Subconscious Motifs |
|---|---|---|
| Strong/Bound | "sombre" | Punching / Pressing |
| Light/Free | "irradiant" | Floating / Flicking |
| Strong/Free | "overpowering" | Wringing / Slashing |
| Light/Bound | "diffused" | Gliding / Dabbing |

**Fusions:**
- Strong/Bound going Strong/Free → Pressing going Slashing
- Light/Bound going Light/Free → Gliding going Flicking

### Feel / posture (pp. 453-460)

> "In Adream, the Character's Intending combines with Adapting (Feeling). As a result the Character's Intending (desires, Objectives and dynamic quality) is buffeted by the storm of his emotions. Yat Malmgren likens the Character to a car driving to a destination — Adream characters career wildly from one side of the road to another, prey to their emotions. But Adream, like an Aston Martin or a Lamborghini is also highly seductive. Adream arouses both sensuously and emotionally." (p. 454)

> "Adream is a composite Inner Attitude, made of Near and Mobile. The essence of Near is Sensing; the essence of Mobile is Feeling. Sensing and Feeling are more often than not in conflict with each other. Indeed, within Adream each tries to get the upper hand and 'expel' the other from the Inner Attitude. Because of this, Adream characters are often torn between two contradictory impulses — one can almost say that they have a dual nature." (p. 454)

Aspects:
- **Enclosing Adream** = Intending dominant — sensuous (Mimi, Cho-Cho-San, Othello, Hedda Gabler)
- **Radiating Adream** = Adapting dominant — feeling type (Desdemona, Hamlet, Ophelia, Richard II, Feste, Orsino, Antonio, Jaques, Sebastian, Viola)

> "It is doubly 'unaware': Adream characters are not only unaware of anything around them, but neither are they interested in becoming aware. Yat Malmgren gives as an example of this attitude to life the type of actor who says: 'I am an actor, but I never go to the theatre'. For Adream characters ignorance is bliss." (p. 455)

### Time-stress vs Space-stress in Adream — the maturity gradient (p. 455-456)

> "Adream characters... have at their disposal two possible Stresses: Time and Space. Immature personalities such as adolescents usually choose the first. A Time-stress causes what Yat Malmgren calls a 'split' in the Inner Attitude of Adream. Under its influence Adream divides into its component Near and Mobile 'simple' Attitudes. These are used by the Time-stressed Adream Character as its Action Attitudes. In doing this, immature characters deny themselves the possibility of Stable and Remote Actions, thus giving up on vision, on ideas. The more sophisticated Adream character uses the Space-stress to create an outer appearance of intelligence, control and aristocratic or intellectual aloofness: Action Attitudes of Stable and Remote." (p. 456)

This is a **major mechanic**: Time-stressed Adream "splits" to Near/Mobile outer; Space-stressed Adream consolidates to Stable/Remote outer. Hamlet (Space-stress) vs Ophelia (Time-stress) — same inner type, very different outer.

> "Yat Malmgren maintains that the more developed (more mature or higher in status) a character is, the more likely it is that it will have a Space-stress. Less developed Near characters are Flow-stressed, while Adream characters which are lower either in intelligence or in social position are Time-stressed." (p. 458 footnote)

### Variations detail

**ADREAM STRONG/BOUND ("sombre")** (p. 460-462):
> "Overpowering, sombre unawareness of a 'staunch resolve' or of an 'aggressive resolve'. Subconscious Motifs: Punching/Pressing."

Pressing more introvert. Lear's curse, Cleopatra "Sir, I will eat no meat...", Timon "Live loathed and long..." — staccato body-blows.
> "the actor should beware of playing the anger: the anger is the inner state, and in order to be dynamic and to avoid self-indulgence, it will have to be translated into transitive Actions" (p. 461)

**ADREAM LIGHT/FREE ("irradiant")** (p. 462-465):
> "Irradiant unfolding or irradiant enfolding of a 'sympathetic exultation'. Motifs: Floating / Flicking."

Look to future / quickening heart. Floating = introvert (reverie); Flicking = extravert. "Unfolding" = energy outward; "enfolding" = energy inward. Phoebe in *As You Like It*: convex Flicking/concave Floating alternation. "Most Time-stressed Light/Free characters are quite stupid, yet come across as charming."
> "Light/Free is a supremely yielding Variation: characters seized by this tempo do not fight against their problems. On the contrary, they yield to overcome an obstacle, they 'stoop to conquer'." (p. 463)

**ADREAM STRONG/FREE ("overpowering")** (p. 465-468):
> "Irradiant intention of 'casting a spell' or of 'being spell-bound'. Motifs: Wringing / Slashing."

Romantic tempo par excellence. Lovborg, Don Carlos, Henry V. Mick Jagger, Nureyev. "Strong and silent type" of Burt Lancaster, Kirk Douglas. Free Flow effect: makes inner energy move in waves; Intending mixes with Flexible (wavering) Attending; "softens sharp corners into round shapes."

> "Strong/Free characters are not particularly intelligent or intuitive, but they have an attractive 'animal' quality. This quality comes across as relaxation — the ability to submit one's body to the force of gravity, of experiencing one's Weight." (p. 466)

Clytemnestra's "I suffered and suffered..." — nine emotional crescendo steps under Free Flow.

**ADREAM LIGHT/BOUND ("diffused")** (p. 468-469):
> "Diffused sensation of a 'welcome feeling' or of an 'irreconcilable feeling'. Motifs: Gliding / Dabbing."

Giggly embarrassment. Hand-over-mouth, hand-wringing in apron, blushing. Welcome feeling = energy inward; irreconcilable feeling = energy outward. "Silly laugh of welcome (extravert) or breaking into a sweat (introvert)." Sir Andrew Aguecheek — "rabbit-like, trying to tiptoe his way out of embarrassment."

### Negatives / drives

> "Adream thus lacks all Intuitive powers. It also lacks Thinking (Attending). It is doubly 'unaware'." (p. 455)

Drive = emotional Objectives (love, vengeance). Adream characters are most central to drama — "our fascination with this potentially explosive combination as well as the uncertainties and personality splits with which our culture is so concerned."

### Lear example — the "swing of the pendulum" between Stresses (pp. 470-473)

This is the multi-Variation / multi-Stress piece. Lear's curse is Adream Strong/Bound. Beforehand he greets Albany with Direct Attending (Adream-with-Awake-flavor) producing Outer Stable/Remote. After the curse, Shakespeare gives him a Stress flip (Time→Space) as Albany speaks. Lear oscillates: Time-stressed fury → Space-stressed reflection → back to fury → Stable inner / Mobile outer. Mirodan: "the swing of the pendulum."

Key observation: *characters don't lock to one Variation*; they typically use **3 of 4** (p. 470).


## 8. Stress mechanics — full treatment

### What "Flow-Stressed Stable" or "Time-Stressed Near" means

Each Inner Attitude that **can** be embodied as a character (the three Sensing-containing ones: Stable, Adream, Near) has **two possible Stress axes** drawn from the two Motion Factors NOT in its inner make-up.

Mirodan's four definitional rules (collected from each chapter's opening definition):

| Inner Attitude | Inner factors | Two possible Stresses |
|---|---|---|
| Stable | Intending + Attending (Weight + Space) | Time-stress OR Flow-stress |
| Adream | Intending + Adapting (Weight + Flow) | Time-stress OR Space-stress |
| Near | Intending + Deciding (Weight + Time) | Space-stress OR Flow-stress |
| Awake | Attending + Deciding (Space + Time) | Weight-stress OR Flow-stress (only as Action Attitude) |
| Remote | Attending + Adapting (Space + Flow) | Weight-stress OR Time-stress (only as Action Attitude) |
| Mobile | Adapting + Deciding (Flow + Time) | Weight-stress OR Space-stress (only as Action Attitude) |

**Key insight**: the Stress axis is always one of the two Motion Factors **missing** from the Inner Attitude's two-factor base. Adding that missing factor to the inner two creates a complete three-factor working state — which IS the Action Attitude (also called Externalized Drive in Chapter III).

### The deterministic mapping rule (per-Attitude tables)

Each of Mirodan's six Attitude chapters opens with a table of the form:
```
[Stress Factor 1] + [Inner Factor A] = [Outer Action Attitude X]
[Stress Factor 1] + [Inner Factor B] = [Outer Action Attitude Y]
[Stress Factor 2] + [Inner Factor A] = [Outer Action Attitude Z]
[Stress Factor 2] + [Inner Factor B] = [Outer Action Attitude W]
```

Wait — that's not quite right. The table actually shows: each of the two Stresses pairs with each of the two Motion Factors NOT in the inner OR pairs with the unused factor. Reading again carefully (Stable example, p. 487):

```
TIME + WEIGHT = NEAR;     AWAKE = TIME + SPACE
FLOW + WEIGHT = ADREAM;   REMOTE = FLOW + SPACE
```

The Stable inner is Weight+Space (Intending+Attending). The two Stresses are Time and Flow. Each Stress combines with **either** the Weight or the Space half of the Stable inner to create one Outer Action Attitude. So Stable produces FOUR possible Outer Action Attitudes (Near, Awake, Adream, Remote) — it is a complete map to all the OTHER Attitudes (excluding its opposite Mobile, which is impossible).

**The deterministic rule**: Stable + Time → Near (if Time joins Weight) OR Awake (if Time joins Space). Stable + Flow → Adream (if Flow joins Weight) OR Remote (if Flow joins Space). 

This means: a Stable character with Time-stress can OUTER-MANIFEST as Near (when emphasizing Weight side) OR as Awake (when emphasizing Space side). The character chooses among them according to the moment's Inner-factor emphasis.

### Stable's complete Stress map

```
Stable inner = Weight + Space
TIME + WEIGHT = NEAR        AWAKE = TIME + SPACE
FLOW + WEIGHT = ADREAM      REMOTE = FLOW + SPACE
```

Time-stressed Stable can outer as: Near or Awake.
Flow-stressed Stable can outer as: Adream or Remote.

### Adream's complete Stress map

```
Adream inner = Weight + Flow
WEIGHT + TIME = NEAR        STABLE = WEIGHT + SPACE
FLOW + TIME = MOBILE        REMOTE = FLOW + SPACE
```

Time-stressed Adream can outer as: Near or Mobile.
Space-stressed Adream can outer as: Stable or Remote.

### Near's complete Stress map

```
Near inner = Weight + Time
SPACE + WEIGHT = STABLE     AWAKE = SPACE + TIME
FLOW + WEIGHT = ADREAM      MOBILE = FLOW + TIME
```

Space-stressed Near can outer as: Stable or Awake.
Flow-stressed Near can outer as: Adream or Mobile.

### Whether stress is permanent (baseline modifier) or episodic (a moment)

**Both.** Mirodan treats stress as **primarily a baseline character trait** ("Big Daddy is an enclosing Flow-stressed Near character" — p. 418), but **both stresses are available** to switch between within a scene.

Critical passage (p. 470, Adream chapter):
> "their Action has at its disposal two Stresses as well and Characters can switch between them within scenes, to create different relationships and different 'beats'."

And the Lear example (p. 472) shows Time-stress→Space-stress mid-scene: "Shakespeare allows him a second opportunity to change from Time-stress to Space-stress." So stress IS episodic — Mirodan calls it "the swing of the pendulum."

But there's a hierarchy: every character has a **Dominant Stress** (Big Daddy = Flow-dominant; Cleopatra = Space-dominant; Lear = Time-dominant). The non-dominant stress is available but emerges in specific scene-moments.

### Sub-axis pole locking under stress (Bound vs Free question)

Mirodan does NOT explicitly say "if Bound-Flow-stressed, character locks to Bound when in outers." She does describe gradient: a Flow-stressed character can be **Bound-Flow-stressed** specifically (e.g. Werle in *The Wild Duck*, p. 491) or **Free-Flow-stressed** (Big Daddy "Flow-stressed character displays a pleasant, sociable, endearing Outer Action. Under the influence of free flowing emotion..." p. 415 footnote 2).

The Stress is not just "Flow" — it's "Bound Flow" or "Free Flow" specifically. Same for Time (Quick or Sustained), Weight (Strong or Light), Space (Direct or Flexible).

But: this is where **Fusions** enter. A character anchored to Bound-Flow-stress can momentarily produce Free-Flow expression via Fusion. So pole-locking is the baseline; Fusion is the documented release valve.

### Maturity/development gradient (p. 458 footnote)

> "Yat Malmgren maintains that the more developed (more mature or higher in status) a character is, the more likely it is that it will have a Space-stress. Less developed Near characters are Flow-stressed, while Adream characters which are lower either in intelligence or in social position are Time-stressed."

So Stress is also a **status indicator**:
- Space-stress = mature, sophisticated, higher status
- Time-stress = adolescent, less developed, lower status
- Flow-stress = emotional, sociable

### Adream's special "split" mechanic (p. 456)

> "A Time-stress causes what Yat Malmgren calls a 'split' in the Inner Attitude of Adream. Under its influence Adream divides into its component Near and Mobile 'simple' Attitudes. These are used by the Time-stressed Adream Character as its Action Attitudes. In doing this, immature characters deny themselves the possibility of Stable and Remote Actions, thus giving up on vision, on ideas. The more sophisticated Adream character uses the Space-stress to create an outer appearance of intelligence, control and aristocratic or intellectual aloofness: Action Attitudes of Stable and Remote."

Critical: the "split" suggests that Time-stressed Adream literally **fragments** to its constituent Near + Mobile, while Space-stressed Adream remains intact and projects unified Stable + Remote outer.

### Stable special "lack" mechanic (p. 489)

Stable is generated by **moving away from Mobility** — a "lack of Mobility." Inversely, Stable's Super-Objective is often the experience of Mobility being kept at bay. When the dam breaks (Creon at his son's death) the suppressed Mobility floods back.

### Awake / Remote / Mobile special role: only-as-Action

> "Awake (like Mobile and Remote), not having Intending as a component, can only exist as an Action Attitude... It cannot exist as an aspect of Adream, its opposite Attitude, with which it is incompatible."

The three opposite-pairs are absolute:
- Adream ↔ Awake (mutually exclusive)
- Near ↔ Remote (mutually exclusive)
- Stable ↔ Mobile (mutually exclusive)

A character anchored at one pole CANNOT have its opposite as either Inner Attitude or Action Attitude. This is the most important hard rule of the Stress mechanics.


## 9. Variations and state transitions

### How Mirodan describes transitioning between full state pairs

Mirodan does NOT describe Stable↔Mobile, Near↔Remote, or Awake↔Adream as transitions. **These are explicitly impossible** within a single character (mutually exclusive opposites).

What CAN transition:
1. **Within the four Variations of an Inner Attitude** — characters use 3 of 4 typically (p. 470). Movement is fluid and untheorized — Mirodan treats this as a continuous palette.
2. **Between Stresses** — Time-stress ↔ Space-stress (Adream); Time ↔ Flow (Stable); Space ↔ Flow (Near). The "swing of the pendulum" (Lear example).
3. **Within the Bound↔Free flow axis** via Fusions — for the three Flow-containing Attitudes (Adream, Mobile, Remote).
4. **Between Outer Action Attitudes** — produced from baseline Inner Attitude + selected Stress. A Stable-baseline character can shift between Near, Awake, Adream, Remote outers depending on which Stress is active and which inner factor it joins.

### The "regression" pattern (p. 458)

> "Most Adream characters are similar to Ophelia: they have 'regressed' to the basic Feeling function, keeping from their full Adream phase, from adolescence, only the memory of Near, reflected in the Time-stress."

This is a **developmental transition** — not within-scene but within-character-history. A character can be locked at a regressed state.

### The "incomplete" / "distorted" Adream pattern (pp. 455, 506)

Mirodan distinguishes "complete" from "incomplete/distorted" Adream characters. Incomplete = robbed of Near (e.g. Ophelia) → behaves as if Mobile but is Adream. This is a transition-by-default, where the character can't access its own full Inner Attitude.

### Within-Variation movement (the standard mode of operation)

This is the bread-and-butter of acting. A Stable character moves through Strong/Direct → Strong/Flexible → Light/Flexible → Light/Direct as the moment demands. Mirodan treats these four as a quadrant the character moves around freely, not as states with discrete transitions.

### Context: Mirodan's grammar of state transitions

The vocabulary Mirodan uses for transitions:
- **"swing of the pendulum"** — between Stresses
- **"going X"** — Fusion (signature mode-shift)
- **"split"** — Time-stressed Adream fragmenting
- **"regression"** — character permanently anchored below its full potential
- **"distortion"** / **"incomplete"** — character missing one of its inner factors
- **"reassert itself"** — a suppressed Inner Participation reemerging (e.g. Near reasserting itself in Lear after fury)
- **"surface"** — unconscious decision becoming conscious (when Deciding emphasized in Near/Mobile/Awake)
- **"break out"** — emotion returning after being held in check

### Mirodan does NOT use:
- "transition" (this is reader's vocabulary)
- "polar visitation"
- "micro-shift"
- "pole inversion"
- "state change"

She works in a continuous metaphor: pendulum swing, palette of Variations, regression/reassertion, dam-and-flood, swing inside the body.


## 10. Action Attitudes (if distinct from Inner)

### Position in the system

Action Attitudes are NOT the same as Inner Attitudes. Mirodan dedicates Chapter II to Inner Attitudes; **Chapter III is "Externalized Drives"** — and Action Attitudes are generated from the interaction between Inner Attitude + Stress.

The mechanic: **Inner Attitude + Stress → Action Attitude**.

> "Mobile, like Awake and Remote, not having Intending as a component, can only exist as an Action Attitude itself... Its own, so-called 'Action Attitudes' occur only as nuances of outer expression. The process of adding stresses and forming outer Attitudes at the level of the Action is described in the chapter on 'The Confluence of the Externalized Drives' below." (p. 503-504)

### Externalized Drives (Chapter III preview, p. 525-530)

Externalized Drives = the four "overwhelming impressions" / qualities that stand out from a character's actions:

| Drive | Quality pair | Mental Factor |
|---|---|---|
| DOING | exerting / reacting | Sensing |
| PASSION | constructing / destroying | Feeling |
| SPELL | dominating / surrendering | Intuiting |
| VISION | ideas / problems | Thinking |

Mirodan's Hamlet-cast example (p. 525-527):
- **Polonius** = Doing (busy man, reacts then exerts)
- **Ophelia** = Passion (buffeted by Passions stronger than herself, "destroys" herself, attempts to "construct" loving relationships)
- **Fortinbras** = Spell (surrenders to the Idea of divine right, then dominates the assembled court)
- **Hamlet** = Vision (turns ideas, sees other side, examines the problem)

### Carpenter's narrow concept vs Yat Malmgren's expansive one (p. 528-529)

**Carpenter**: Externalized Drives are by-products of Inner Attitudes — "a transmission belt between these and the Action."

**Yat Malmgren**: Externalized Drives are PRIOR to Inner Attitudes — they motivate the formation of Inner Attitudes from Super-Objectives. The chain:

```
PSYCHOLOGICAL TYPE → SUPER-OBJECTIVE → EXTERNALIZED DRIVES (INNER) →
→ INNER ATTITUDE → EXTERNALIZED DRIVES (OUTER) → ACTION ATTITUDES
```

So Externalized Drives have a **dual function**: into the Inner Attitude come Drives; out of it depart other Drives moving to Action.

Mirodan's synthesis: "Externalized Drives are a unifying force, joining the Character with his Actions... a bridge made of energy, or perhaps a spurt of energy transmitting intentions from Super Objective to Character to Action." (p. 530)

### Key structural difference

> "The Externalized Drives are expressed 'fully' through three Motion Factors creating complete Working Actions. The Inner Attitudes, on the other hand are only expressed through two 'incomplete' Motion Factors, in the form of Shadow Moves." (p. 530)

So:
- Inner Attitude = 2 Motion Factors → Shadow Move (subliminal)
- Action Attitude / Externalized Drive = 3 Motion Factors → full Working Action (visible)

The Stress is the **third Motion Factor** that lifts a 2-factor Inner Attitude into a 3-factor visible Action.

### Note: this section bleeds into Chapter III

The full treatment of Action Attitudes / Externalized Drives is in Chapter III ("Externalized Drives"), beyond the scope of this Chapter II extraction. Chapter II ends at printed p. 524.


## 11. Surprises / overrides

### Big surprise #1: "Fusions" is the canonical term, NOT "polar visitation"

The user's framing — "you can go free if you're mostly bound. In moments. Bound going free." — is exactly Mirodan's mechanic, and her vocabulary is **"Fusions"** with the verb form **"X going Y."** (p. 449-450 etc.) Critically, this is NOT a state change or character-flexibility — Mirodan's framing is severe:

> "Fusions are appearances of Free Flow covering a Bound Flow inner. A character in Fusion tries to convince others that he has Free Flow, but in reality he is extremely Bound and that is apparent to everyone except himself." (p. 449-450)

**The Free pole is FAKE. The Bound pole is the truth. The Fusion is transparent self-deception visible to everyone except the character.**

### Big surprise #2: Fusions only apply to Flow-Attitudes

Fusions are ONLY documented for Adream, Mobile, and Remote (the three Inner Attitudes containing Flow). NOT for Stable, Near, or Awake. So the Bound↔Free axis is the only documented "polar visitation" axis.

Strong↔Light, Direct↔Flexible, Quick↔Sustained — these have no Fusion equivalent in Mirodan. The character moves around them within the Variation palette but doesn't have a documented "going Y" mechanic.

### Big surprise #3: Three Inner Attitudes can never be "Characters"

Awake, Remote, and Mobile **cannot exist as Inner Attitudes of a character** — they exist only as **Action Attitudes** (Outer projections) of Stable / Adream / Near baseline characters. This is because they don't contain Intending (Weight). A character must contain Intending to be physical/embodied.

Practical consequence: any reference to "Hamlet is Mobile" or "Macbeth is Awake" is shorthand. Hamlet is Adream; Macbeth is Adream-Sensing-type. Their Mobile/Awake/Remote moments are Action Attitudes / Outer projections.

### Big surprise #4: Mutually-exclusive opposite pairs

The three opposite pairs are absolute:
- Stable ↔ Mobile (cannot coexist)
- Near ↔ Remote (cannot coexist)
- Awake ↔ Adream (cannot coexist)

A character anchored to one pole CANNOT have its opposite. This is the strongest hard rule in the system.

### Big surprise #5: Characters use 3 of 4 Variations, not 1

> "most characters use at least three of the four 'tempi' open to them at the level of the Inner Attitude. Moreover, their Action has at its disposal two Stresses as well and Characters can switch between them within scenes, to create different relationships and different 'beats'." (p. 470)

A character is not locked to one Variation — it has a quadrant available, plus both Stresses to switch between. The "fourth" variation is typically the one explicitly avoided (Johnny Boyle never uses Quick/Free because it would mean welcoming death).

### Big surprise #6: Stress is BOTH baseline AND episodic

Each character has a **dominant** Stress (a baseline trait — "Big Daddy is Flow-stressed," "Cleopatra is Space-stressed"), but **both** Stresses are available, switchable mid-scene. The dominant determines the typical projection; the secondary creates "beats."

### Big surprise #7: The Time/Space stress as maturity indicator (Adream)

> "the more developed (more mature or higher in status) a character is, the more likely it is that it will have a Space-stress. Less developed Near characters are Flow-stressed, while Adream characters which are lower either in intelligence or in social position are Time-stressed." (p. 458 footnote)

Stress is a **status grammar** — Time = adolescent / lower; Space = mature / higher; Flow = emotional / sociable.

### Big surprise #8: "Adream split" mechanic

Time-stressed Adream is unique: it doesn't just produce Time-modulated outputs, it **splits** into its constituent Near + Mobile. So a Time-stressed Adream character flickers between two simpler outers, while a Space-stressed Adream consolidates into Stable + Remote. (p. 456)

### Big surprise #9: Aspect (enclosing/penetrating/circumscribing/radiating) cuts orthogonally to Stress

Each Inner Attitude has TWO Aspects (its emphasis pattern):
- Intending emphasized = **enclosing**
- Attending emphasized = **penetrating**
- Deciding emphasized = **circumscribing**
- Adapting emphasized = **radiating**

Aspect is fixed per character (Othello = enclosing Adream; Desdemona = radiating Adream). Stress is more flexible. So a character has: [Inner Attitude] × [Aspect, fixed] × [Dominant Stress, mostly fixed] × [available Variations within].

### Big surprise #10: The Stress is the THIRD Motion Factor

Stress isn't an extra thing — it's the **third Motion Factor** that completes a 2-factor Inner Attitude into a 3-factor visible Action. Inner Attitudes are Shadow Moves (2 factors); Externalized Drives / Action Attitudes are Working Actions (3 factors). Stress is the bridge.
